title: Outer mitochondrial membrane

infobox.value: (pl. **mitochondria**) is an [organelle](/wiki/Organelle "Organelle") found in the [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") of most [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), such as [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal"), [plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant") and [fungi](/wiki/Fungi "Fungi"). Mitochondria have a double [membrane](/wiki/Lipid_bilayer "Lipid bilayer") structure and use [aerobic respiration](/wiki/Aerobic_respiration "Aerobic respiration") to generate [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (ATP), which is used throughout the cell as a source of [chemical energy](/wiki/Chemical_energy "Chemical energy").[[2]](#cite_note-2) They were discovered by [Albert von Kölliker](/wiki/Albert_von_K%C3%B6lliker "Albert von Kölliker") in 1857[[3]](#cite_note-Science_in_the_News-2012-3) in the voluntary muscles of insects. The term *mitochondrion*, meaning a thread-like granule, was coined by [Carl Benda](/wiki/Carl_Benda "Carl Benda") in 1898. The mitochondrion is popularly nicknamed the "powerhouse of the cell", a phrase popularized by [Philip Siekevitz](/wiki/Philip_Siekevitz "Philip Siekevitz") in a 1957 *[Scientific American](/wiki/Scientific_American "Scientific American")* article of the same name.[[4]](#cite_note-Siekevitz-1957-4)

A **mitochondrion** (pl. **mitochondria**) is an [organelle](/wiki/Organelle "Organelle") found in the [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") of most [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), such as [animals](/wiki/Animal "Animal"), [plants](/wiki/Plant "Plant") and [fungi](/wiki/Fungi "Fungi"). Mitochondria have a double [membrane](/wiki/Lipid_bilayer "Lipid bilayer") structure and use [aerobic respiration](/wiki/Aerobic_respiration "Aerobic respiration") to generate [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (ATP), which is used throughout the cell as a source of [chemical energy](/wiki/Chemical_energy "Chemical energy").[[2]](#cite_note-2) They were discovered by [Albert von Kölliker](/wiki/Albert_von_K%C3%B6lliker "Albert von Kölliker") in 1857[[3]](#cite_note-Science_in_the_News-2012-3) in the voluntary muscles of insects. The term *mitochondrion*, meaning a thread-like granule, was coined by [Carl Benda](/wiki/Carl_Benda "Carl Benda") in 1898. The mitochondrion is popularly nicknamed the "powerhouse of the cell", a phrase popularized by [Philip Siekevitz](/wiki/Philip_Siekevitz "Philip Siekevitz") in a 1957 *[Scientific American](/wiki/Scientific_American "Scientific American")* article of the same name.[[4]](#cite_note-Siekevitz-1957-4)

Some cells in some [multicellular organisms](/wiki/Multicellular_organism "Multicellular organism") lack mitochondria (for example, mature mammalian [red blood cells](/wiki/Red_blood_cell "Red blood cell")). The multicellular [animal](/wiki/Animal "Animal") *[Henneguya salminicola](/wiki/Henneguya_zschokkei "Henneguya zschokkei")* is known to have retained mitochondrion-related organelles despite a complete loss of their mitochondrial genome.[[5]](#cite_note-Karnkowska-2016-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Le_Page-2020-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Yahalomi-2020-7) A large number of [unicellular organisms](/wiki/Unicellular_organism "Unicellular organism"), such as [microsporidia](/wiki/Microsporidia "Microsporidia"), [parabasalids](/wiki/Parabasalid "Parabasalid") and [diplomonads](/wiki/Diplomonad "Diplomonad"), have reduced or transformed their mitochondria into other structures,[[8]](#cite_note-Henze-2003-8) e.g. [hydrogenosomes](/wiki/Hydrogenosome "Hydrogenosome") and [mitosomes](/wiki/Mitosome "Mitosome").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The [oxymonads](/wiki/Oxymonad "Oxymonad") *[Monocercomonoides](/wiki/Monocercomonoides "Monocercomonoides")*, *[Streblomastix](/wiki/Streblomastix "Streblomastix")*, and *Blattamonas* completely lost their mitochondria.[[5]](#cite_note-Karnkowska-2016-5)[[10]](#cite_note-Novák-2023-10)

Mitochondria are commonly between 0.75 and 3 [μm](/wiki/Micrometre "Micrometre")2 in cross section,[[11]](#cite_note-Wiemerslage-2016-11) but vary considerably in size and structure. Unless specifically [stained](/wiki/Staining "Staining"), they are not visible. The mitochondrion is composed of compartments that carry out specialized functions. These compartments or regions include the outer membrane, [intermembrane space](/wiki/Intermembrane_space "Intermembrane space"), [inner membrane](/wiki/Inner_mitochondrial_membrane "Inner mitochondrial membrane"), [cristae](/wiki/Crista "Crista"), and [matrix](/wiki/Mitochondrial_matrix "Mitochondrial matrix").

In addition to supplying cellular energy, mitochondria are involved in other tasks, such as [signaling](/wiki/Cell_signaling "Cell signaling"), [cellular differentiation](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation"), and [cell death](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis"), as well as maintaining control of the [cell cycle](/wiki/Cell_cycle "Cell cycle") and [cell growth](/wiki/Cell_growth "Cell growth").[[12]](#cite_note-McBride-2006-12) [Mitochondrial biogenesis](/wiki/Mitochondrial_biogenesis "Mitochondrial biogenesis") is in turn temporally coordinated with these cellular processes.[[13]](#cite_note-Valero-2014-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Sanchis-Gomar-2014-14)

Mitochondria are implicated in human disorders and conditions such as [mitochondrial diseases](/wiki/Mitochondrial_disease "Mitochondrial disease"),[[15]](#cite_note-15) [cardiac dysfunction](/wiki/Heart_failure "Heart failure"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) heart failure,[[17]](#cite_note-Dorn-2015-17) and [autism](/wiki/Autism "Autism").[[18]](#cite_note-Griffiths-2017-18)

The number of mitochondria in a cell vary widely by [organism](/wiki/Organism "Organism"), [tissue](/wiki/Tissue_(biology) "Tissue (biology)"), and cell type. A mature red blood cell has no mitochondria,[[19]](#cite_note-Ney-2011-19) whereas a [liver cell](/wiki/Hepatocyte "Hepatocyte") can have more than 2000.[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21)

Although most of a [eukaryotic cell](/wiki/Eukaryotic_cell "Eukaryotic cell")'s [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") is contained in the [cell nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"), the mitochondrion has [its own genome](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") ("mitogenome") that is similar to [bacterial](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") genomes.[[22]](#cite_note-22) This finding has led to general acceptance of [symbiogenesis](/wiki/Symbiogenesis "Symbiogenesis") (endosymbiotic theory) – that free-living prokaryotic ancestors of modern mitochondria permanently fused with eukaryotic cells in the distant past, evolving such that modern animals, plants, fungi, and other eukaryotes [respire](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration") to generate [cellular energy](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate").[[23]](#cite_note-Gabaldón-2021-23)

## Structure

Mitochondria may have a number of different shapes.[[24]](#cite_note-British_Society_for_Cell_Biology-24) A mitochondrion contains outer and inner membranes composed of [phospholipid bilayers](/wiki/Phospholipid_bilayer "Phospholipid bilayer") and [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein").[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20) The two membranes have different properties. Because of this double-membraned organization, there are five distinct parts to a mitochondrion:

1. The outer mitochondrial membrane,
2. The intermembrane space (the space between the outer and inner membranes),
3. The inner mitochondrial membrane,
4. The [cristae](/wiki/Crista "Crista") space (formed by infoldings of the inner membrane), and
5. The [matrix](/wiki/Mitochondrial_matrix "Mitochondrial matrix") (space within the inner membrane), which is a fluid.

Mitochondria have folding to increase surface area, which in turn increases ATP (adenosine triphosphate) production.
Mitochondria stripped of their outer membrane are called [mitoplasts](/wiki/Mitoplast "Mitoplast").

### Outer membrane

The **outer mitochondrial membrane**, which encloses the entire organelle, is 60 to 75 [angstroms](/wiki/Angstrom "Angstrom") (Å) thick. It has a protein-to-phospholipid ratio similar to that of the [cell membrane](/wiki/Cell_membrane "Cell membrane") (about 1:1 by weight). It contains large numbers of [integral membrane proteins](/wiki/Integral_membrane_protein "Integral membrane protein") called [porins](/wiki/Porin_(protein) "Porin (protein)"). A major trafficking protein is the pore-forming [voltage-dependent anion channel](/wiki/Voltage-dependent_anion_channel "Voltage-dependent anion channel") (VDAC). The [VDAC](/wiki/Voltage-dependent_anion_channel#Biological_function "Voltage-dependent anion channel") is the primary transporter of [nucleotides](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide"), [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion") and [metabolites](/wiki/Metabolite "Metabolite") between the [cytosol](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol") and the intermembrane space.[[25]](#cite_note-Blachly-Dyson-2001-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Hoogenboom-2007-26) It is formed as a [beta barrel](/wiki/Beta_barrel "Beta barrel") that spans the outer membrane, similar to that in the [gram-negative](/wiki/Gram-negative "Gram-negative") [bacterial outer membrane](/wiki/Bacterial_outer_membrane "Bacterial outer membrane").[[27]](#cite_note-Zeth-2010-27) Larger proteins can enter the mitochondrion if a signaling sequence at their [N-terminus](/wiki/N-terminus "N-terminus") binds to a large multisubunit [protein](/wiki/Mitochondrial_membrane_transport_protein "Mitochondrial membrane transport protein") called [translocase in the outer membrane](/wiki/Translocase_of_the_outer_membrane "Translocase of the outer membrane"), which then [actively moves](/wiki/Active_transport "Active transport") them across the membrane.[[28]](#cite_note-Herrmann-2000-28) Mitochondrial [pro-proteins](/wiki/Protein_precursor "Protein precursor") are imported through specialised translocation complexes.

The outer membrane also contains [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") involved in such diverse activities as the elongation of [fatty acids](/wiki/Fatty_acid "Fatty acid"), [oxidation](/wiki/Oxidation "Oxidation") of [epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine"), and the [degradation](/wiki/Biodegradation "Biodegradation") of [tryptophan](/wiki/Tryptophan "Tryptophan"). These enzymes include [monoamine oxidase](/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase "Monoamine oxidase"), [rotenone](/wiki/Rotenone "Rotenone")-insensitive NADH-cytochrome c-reductase, [kynurenine](/wiki/Kynurenine "Kynurenine") [hydroxylase](/wiki/Hydroxylase "Hydroxylase") and fatty acid Co-A [ligase](/wiki/Ligase "Ligase"). Disruption of the outer membrane permits proteins in the intermembrane space to leak into the cytosol, leading to cell death.[[29]](#cite_note-Chipuk-2006-29) The outer mitochondrial membrane can associate with the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) membrane, in a structure called MAM (mitochondria-associated ER-membrane). This is important in the ER-mitochondria calcium signaling and is involved in the transfer of lipids between the ER and mitochondria.[[30]](#cite_note-Hayashi-2009-30) Outside the outer membrane are small (diameter: 60 Å) particles named sub-units of Parson.

### Intermembrane space

The **mitochondrial intermembrane space** is the space between the outer membrane and the inner membrane. It is also known as perimitochondrial space. Because the outer membrane is freely permeable to small molecules, the concentrations of small molecules, such as ions and sugars, in the intermembrane space is the same as in the [cytosol](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol").[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20) However, large proteins must have a specific signaling sequence to be transported across the outer membrane, so the protein composition of this space is different from the protein composition of the [cytosol](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol"). One [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") that is localized to the intermembrane space in this way is [cytochrome c](/wiki/Cytochrome_c "Cytochrome c").[[29]](#cite_note-Chipuk-2006-29)

### Inner membrane

Main article: [Inner mitochondrial membrane](/wiki/Inner_mitochondrial_membrane "Inner mitochondrial membrane")

The inner mitochondrial membrane contains proteins with three types of functions:[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20)

1. Those that perform the [electron transport chain](/wiki/Electron_transport_chain "Electron transport chain") [redox](/wiki/Redox "Redox") reactions
2. [ATP synthase](/wiki/ATP_synthase "ATP synthase"), which generates [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") in the matrix
3. Specific [transport proteins](/wiki/Membrane_transport_protein "Membrane transport protein") that regulate [metabolite](/wiki/Metabolite "Metabolite") passage into and out of the [mitochondrial matrix](/wiki/Mitochondrial_matrix "Mitochondrial matrix")

It contains more than 151 different [polypeptides](/wiki/Polypeptide "Polypeptide"), and has a very high protein-to-phospholipid ratio (more than 3:1 by weight, which is about 1 protein for 15 phospholipids). The inner membrane is home to around 1/5 of the total protein in a mitochondrion.[[31]](#cite_note-Schenkel-2014-31) Additionally, the inner membrane is rich in an unusual phospholipid, [cardiolipin](/wiki/Cardiolipin "Cardiolipin"). This phospholipid was originally discovered in [cow](/wiki/Bos_taurus "Bos taurus") hearts in 1942, and is usually characteristic of mitochondrial and bacterial plasma membranes.[[32]](#cite_note-McMillin-2002-32) Cardiolipin contains four fatty acids rather than two, and may help to make the inner membrane impermeable,[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20) and its disruption can lead to multiple clinical disorders including neurological disorders and cancer.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Unlike the outer membrane, the inner membrane does not contain porins, and is highly impermeable to all molecules. Almost all ions and molecules require special membrane transporters to enter or exit the matrix. Proteins are ferried into the matrix via the [translocase of the inner membrane](/wiki/Translocase_of_the_inner_membrane "Translocase of the inner membrane") (TIM) complex or via [OXA1L](/wiki/OXA1L "OXA1L").[[28]](#cite_note-Herrmann-2000-28) In addition, there is a membrane potential across the inner membrane, formed by the action of the [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") of the [electron transport chain](/wiki/Electron_transport_chain "Electron transport chain"). Inner membrane [fusion](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fusion "Mitochondrial fusion") is mediated by the inner membrane protein [OPA1](/wiki/OPA1 "OPA1").[[34]](#cite_note-Youle-2012-34)

#### Cristae

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/MitochondrionCAM.jpg/250px-MitochondrionCAM.jpg)](/wiki/File:MitochondrionCAM.jpg)

Cross-sectional image of cristae in a [rat](/wiki/Rat "Rat") liver mitochondrion to demonstrate the likely 3D structure and relationship to the inner membrane

Main article: [Crista](/wiki/Crista "Crista")

The inner mitochondrial membrane is compartmentalized into numerous folds called [cristae](/wiki/Crista "Crista"), which expand the surface area of the inner mitochondrial membrane, enhancing its ability to produce ATP. For typical liver mitochondria, the area of the inner membrane is about five times as large as that of the outer membrane. This ratio is variable and mitochondria from cells that have a greater demand for ATP, such as muscle cells, contain even more cristae. Mitochondria within the same cell can have substantially different crista-density, with the ones that are required to produce more energy having much more crista-membrane surface.[[35]](#cite_note-Cserép-2018-35) These folds are studded with small round bodies known as [F1 particles](/wiki/F-ATPase "F-ATPase") or oxysomes.[[36]](#cite_note-Mannella-2006-36)

### Matrix

Main article: [Mitochondrial matrix](/wiki/Mitochondrial_matrix "Mitochondrial matrix")

The matrix is the space enclosed by the inner membrane. It contains about 2/3 of the total proteins in a mitochondrion.[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20) The matrix is important in the production of ATP with the aid of the ATP synthase contained in the inner membrane. The matrix contains a highly concentrated mixture of hundreds of enzymes, special [mitochondrial ribosomes](/wiki/Mitochondrial_ribosome "Mitochondrial ribosome"), [tRNA](/wiki/TRNA "TRNA"), and several copies of the [mitochondrial DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") [genome](/wiki/Genome "Genome"). Of the enzymes, the major functions include oxidation of [pyruvate](/wiki/Pyruvate "Pyruvate") and [fatty acids](/wiki/Fatty_acids "Fatty acids"), and the [citric acid cycle](/wiki/Citric_acid_cycle "Citric acid cycle").[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20) The DNA molecules are packaged into nucleoids by proteins, one of which is [TFAM](/wiki/TFAM "TFAM").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Function

The most prominent roles of mitochondria are to produce the energy currency of the cell, [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (i.e., phosphorylation of [ADP](/wiki/Adenosine_diphosphate "Adenosine diphosphate")), through respiration and to regulate cellular [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism").[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21) The central set of reactions involved in ATP production are collectively known as the [citric acid cycle](/wiki/Citric_acid_cycle "Citric acid cycle"), or the [Krebs](/wiki/Hans_Adolf_Krebs "Hans Adolf Krebs") cycle, and [oxidative phosphorylation](/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation "Oxidative phosphorylation"). However, the mitochondrion has many other functions in addition to the production of ATP.

### Energy conversion

A dominant role for the mitochondria is the production of ATP, as reflected by the large number of proteins in the inner membrane for this task. This is done by oxidizing the major products of [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose"): [pyruvate](/wiki/Pyruvate "Pyruvate"), and [NADH](/wiki/NADH "NADH"), which are produced in the cytosol.[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21) This type of [cellular respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration"), known as [aerobic respiration](/wiki/Aerobic_respiration "Aerobic respiration"), is dependent on the presence of [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"). When oxygen is limited, the glycolytic products will be metabolized by [anaerobic fermentation](/wiki/Fermentation_(biochemistry) "Fermentation (biochemistry)"), a process that is independent of the mitochondria.[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21) The production of ATP from glucose and oxygen has an approximately 13-times higher yield during aerobic respiration compared to fermentation.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Plant mitochondria can also produce a limited amount of ATP either by breaking the sugar produced during photosynthesis or without oxygen by using the alternate substrate [nitrite](/wiki/Nitrite "Nitrite").[[39]](#cite_note-Stoimenova-2007-39) ATP crosses out through the inner membrane with the help of a [specific protein](/wiki/ATP%E2%80%93ADP_translocase "ATP–ADP translocase"), and across the outer membrane via [porins](/wiki/Porin_(protein) "Porin (protein)").[[40]](#cite_note-Neupert-1997-40) After conversion of ATP to [ADP](/wiki/Adenosine_diphosphate "Adenosine diphosphate") by [dephosphorylation](/wiki/Dephosphorylation "Dephosphorylation") that releases energy, ADP returns via the same route.

#### Pyruvate and the citric acid cycle

Main article: [Citric acid cycle](/wiki/Citric_acid_cycle "Citric acid cycle")

[Pyruvate](/wiki/Pyruvate "Pyruvate") molecules produced by [glycolysis](/wiki/Glycolysis "Glycolysis") are [actively transported](/wiki/Active_transport "Active transport") across the inner mitochondrial membrane, and into the matrix where they can either be [oxidized](/wiki/Redox "Redox") and combined with [coenzyme A](/wiki/Coenzyme_A "Coenzyme A") to form CO2, [acetyl-CoA](/wiki/Acetyl-CoA "Acetyl-CoA"), and [NADH](/wiki/NADH "NADH"),[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21) or they can be [carboxylated](/wiki/Carboxylated "Carboxylated") (by [pyruvate carboxylase](/wiki/Pyruvate_carboxylase "Pyruvate carboxylase")) to form oxaloacetate. This latter reaction "fills up" the amount of oxaloacetate in the citric acid cycle and is therefore an [anaplerotic reaction](/wiki/Anaplerotic_reaction "Anaplerotic reaction"), increasing the cycle's capacity to metabolize acetyl-CoA when the tissue's energy needs (e.g., in [muscle](/wiki/Striated_muscle_tissue "Striated muscle tissue")) are suddenly increased by activity.[[41]](#cite_note-Stryer-1995-41)

In the citric acid cycle, all the intermediates (e.g. [citrate](/wiki/Citrate "Citrate"), [iso-citrate](/wiki/Isocitric_acid "Isocitric acid"), [alpha-ketoglutarate](/wiki/Alpha-Ketoglutaric_acid "Alpha-Ketoglutaric acid"), [Succinyl-CoA](/wiki/Succinyl-CoA "Succinyl-CoA"), succinate, [fumarate](/wiki/Fumaric_acid "Fumaric acid"), [malate](/wiki/Malic_acid "Malic acid") and oxaloacetate) are regenerated during each turn of the cycle. Adding more of any of these intermediates to the mitochondrion therefore means that the additional amount is retained within the cycle, increasing all the other intermediates as one is converted into the other. Hence, the addition of any one of them to the cycle has an [anaplerotic](/wiki/Anaplerotic_reactions "Anaplerotic reactions") effect, and its removal has a cataplerotic effect. These anaplerotic and [cataplerotic](/w/index.php?title=Cataplerotic&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cataplerotic (page does not exist)") reactions will, during the course of the cycle, increase or decrease the amount of oxaloacetate available to combine with acetyl-CoA to form citric acid. This in turn increases or decreases the rate of [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") production by the mitochondrion, and thus the availability of ATP to the cell.[[41]](#cite_note-Stryer-1995-41)

Acetyl-CoA, on the other hand, derived from pyruvate oxidation, or from the [beta-oxidation](/wiki/Beta-oxidation "Beta-oxidation") of [fatty acids](/wiki/Fatty_acids "Fatty acids"), is the only fuel to enter the citric acid cycle. With each turn of the cycle one molecule of acetyl-CoA is consumed for every molecule of oxaloacetate present in the mitochondrial matrix, and is never regenerated. It is the oxidation of the acetate portion of acetyl-CoA that produces CO2 and water, with the energy thus released captured in the form of ATP.[[41]](#cite_note-Stryer-1995-41)

In the liver, the [carboxylation](/wiki/Carboxylation "Carboxylation") of [cytosolic](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol") pyruvate into intra-mitochondrial oxaloacetate is an early step in the [gluconeogenic](/wiki/Gluconeogenesis "Gluconeogenesis") pathway, which converts [lactate](/wiki/Lactic_acid "Lactic acid") and de-aminated [alanine](/wiki/Alanine "Alanine") into glucose,[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21)[[41]](#cite_note-Stryer-1995-41) under the influence of high levels of [glucagon](/wiki/Glucagon "Glucagon") and/or [epinephrine](/wiki/Epinephrine "Epinephrine") in the blood.[[41]](#cite_note-Stryer-1995-41) Here, the addition of oxaloacetate to the mitochondrion does not have a net anaplerotic effect, as another citric acid cycle intermediate (malate) is immediately removed from the mitochondrion to be converted to cytosolic oxaloacetate, and ultimately to glucose, in a process that is almost the reverse of [glycolysis](/wiki/Glycolysis "Glycolysis").[[41]](#cite_note-Stryer-1995-41)

The enzymes of the citric acid cycle are located in the mitochondrial matrix, with the exception of [succinate dehydrogenase](/wiki/Succinate_dehydrogenase "Succinate dehydrogenase"), which is bound to the inner mitochondrial membrane as part of Complex II.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The citric acid cycle oxidizes the acetyl-CoA to carbon dioxide, and, in the process, produces reduced cofactors (three molecules of [NADH](/wiki/NADH "NADH") and one molecule of [FADH2](/wiki/FADH2 "FADH2")) that are a source of electrons for the [electron transport chain](/wiki/Electron_transport_chain "Electron transport chain"), and a molecule of [GTP](/wiki/Guanosine_triphosphate "Guanosine triphosphate") (which is readily converted to an ATP).[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21)

#### O2 and NADH: energy-releasing reactions

Main articles: [Oxidative phosphorylation](/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation "Oxidative phosphorylation") and [electron transport chain](/wiki/Electron_transport_chain "Electron transport chain")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Electron_transport_chain.svg/500px-Electron_transport_chain.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Electron_transport_chain.svg)

Electron transport chain in the mitochondrial intermembrane space

The electrons from NADH and FADH2 are transferred to oxygen (O2) and hydrogen (protons) in several steps via an electron transport chain. NADH and FADH2 molecules are produced within the matrix via the citric acid cycle and in the cytoplasm by [glycolysis](/wiki/Glycolysis "Glycolysis"). [Reducing equivalents](/wiki/Reducing_equivalent "Reducing equivalent") from the cytoplasm can be imported via the [malate-aspartate shuttle](/wiki/Malate-aspartate_shuttle "Malate-aspartate shuttle") system of [antiporter](/wiki/Antiporter "Antiporter") proteins or fed into the electron transport chain using a [glycerol phosphate shuttle](/wiki/Glycerol_phosphate_shuttle "Glycerol phosphate shuttle").[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21)

The major energy-releasing reactions[[43]](#cite_note-Voet-2004-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Atkins-2006-44) that make the mitochondrion the "powerhouse of the cell" occur at [protein complexes I, III and IV](/wiki/Electron_transport_chain#Mitochondrial_redox_carriers "Electron transport chain") in the inner mitochondrial membrane ([NADH dehydrogenase (ubiquinone)](/wiki/NADH_dehydrogenase_(ubiquinone) "NADH dehydrogenase (ubiquinone)"), [cytochrome c reductase](/wiki/Coenzyme_Q_-_cytochrome_c_reductase "Coenzyme Q - cytochrome c reductase"), and [cytochrome c oxidase](/wiki/Cytochrome_c_oxidase "Cytochrome c oxidase")). At [complex IV](/wiki/Complex_IV "Complex IV"), O2 reacts with the reduced form of iron in [cytochrome c](/wiki/Cytochrome_c "Cytochrome c"):

:   O2 + 4 H+(aq) + 4 Fe2+(cyt c) → 2 H2O + 4 Fe3+(cyt c)    ΔrGo' = -218 kJ/mol

releasing a lot of [free energy](/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy")[[44]](#cite_note-Atkins-2006-44)[[43]](#cite_note-Voet-2004-43) from the reactants without breaking bonds of an organic fuel. The free energy put in to remove an electron from Fe2+ is released at [complex III](/wiki/Complex_III "Complex III") when Fe3+ of cytochrome c reacts to oxidize [ubiquinol](/wiki/Ubiquinol "Ubiquinol") (QH2):

:   2 Fe3+(cyt c) + QH2 → 2 Fe2+(cyt c) + Q + 2 H+(aq)    ΔrGo' = -30 kJ/mol

The [ubiquinone](/wiki/Ubiquinone "Ubiquinone") (Q) generated reacts, in [complex I](/wiki/Complex_I "Complex I"), with NADH:

:   Q + H+(aq) + NADH → QH2 + NAD+    ΔrGo' = -81 kJ/mol

While the reactions are controlled by an electron transport chain, free electrons are not amongst the reactants or products in the three reactions shown and therefore do not affect the free energy released, which is used to pump [protons](/wiki/Hydrogen_ion "Hydrogen ion") (H+) into the intermembrane space. This process is efficient, but a small percentage of electrons may prematurely reduce oxygen, forming [reactive oxygen species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species") such as [superoxide](/wiki/Superoxide "Superoxide").[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21) This can cause [oxidative stress](/wiki/Oxidative_stress "Oxidative stress") in the mitochondria and may contribute to the decline in mitochondrial function associated with aging.[[45]](#cite_note-Huang-2004-45)

As the proton concentration increases in the intermembrane space, a strong [electrochemical gradient](/wiki/Electrochemical_gradient "Electrochemical gradient") is established across the inner membrane. The protons can return to the matrix through the [ATP synthase](/wiki/ATP_synthase "ATP synthase") complex, and their potential energy is used to synthesize [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") from ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi).[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21) This process is called [chemiosmosis](/wiki/Chemiosmosis "Chemiosmosis"), and was first described by [Peter Mitchell](/wiki/Peter_D._Mitchell "Peter D. Mitchell"),[[46]](#cite_note-Mitchell-1967a-46)[[47]](#cite_note-Mitchell-1967b-47) who was awarded the 1978 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") for his work. Later, part of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to [Paul D. Boyer](/wiki/Paul_D._Boyer "Paul D. Boyer") and [John E. Walker](/wiki/John_E._Walker "John E. Walker") for their clarification of the working mechanism of ATP synthase.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

#### Heat production

Under certain conditions, protons can re-enter the mitochondrial matrix without contributing to ATP synthesis. This process is known as *proton leak* or [mitochondrial uncoupling](/wiki/Mitochondrial_uncoupling "Mitochondrial uncoupling") and is due to the [facilitated diffusion](/wiki/Facilitated_diffusion "Facilitated diffusion") of protons into the matrix. The process results in the unharnessed potential energy of the proton [electrochemical](/wiki/Electrochemistry "Electrochemistry") gradient being released as heat.[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21) The process is mediated by a proton channel called [thermogenin](/wiki/Thermogenin "Thermogenin"), or [UCP1](/wiki/UCP1 "UCP1").[[49]](#cite_note-Mozo-2005-49) Thermogenin is primarily found in [brown adipose tissue](/wiki/Brown_adipose_tissue "Brown adipose tissue"), or brown fat, and is responsible for non-shivering thermogenesis. Brown adipose tissue is found in mammals, and is at its highest levels in early life and in hibernating animals. In humans, brown adipose tissue is present at birth and decreases with age.[[49]](#cite_note-Mozo-2005-49)

### Mitochondrial fatty acid synthesis

Main article: [Mitochondrial fatty acid synthesis](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fatty_acid_synthesis "Mitochondrial fatty acid synthesis")

Mitochondrial fatty acid synthesis (mtFAS) is essential for cellular respiration and mitochondrial biogenesis.[[50]](#cite_note-50) In response to mitochondrial acetyl-CoA availability, mtFAS builds acyl chains on the [4'-phosphopantetheine](/wiki/4%27-phosphopantetheine "4'-phosphopantetheine") group of the matrix-soluble scaffold protein [ACP](/wiki/Acyl_carrier_protein "Acyl carrier protein") (holo-ACP), producing [acyl-ACP](/w/index.php?title=Acyl-ACP&action=edit&redlink=1 "Acyl-ACP (page does not exist)") species with varying chain lengths of at least eight carbons.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Among these, [octanoyl-ACP](/wiki/Octanoyl-ACP "Octanoyl-ACP") (C8) serves as the precursor for [lipoic acid](/wiki/Lipoic_acid "Lipoic acid") biosynthesis.[[52]](#cite_note-Nowinski-2018-52) Since lipoic acid is a cofactor for key mitochondrial enzyme complexes, including the [pyruvate dehydrogenase complex](/wiki/Pyruvate_dehydrogenase_complex "Pyruvate dehydrogenase complex") (PDC), [α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex](/wiki/Oxoglutarate_dehydrogenase_complex "Oxoglutarate dehydrogenase complex") (OGDC), [2-oxoadipate dehydrogenase complex](/wiki/2-oxoadipate_dehydrogenase_complex "2-oxoadipate dehydrogenase complex") (OADHC), [branched-chain α-ketoacid dehydrogenase complex](/wiki/Branched-chain_alpha-keto_acid_dehydrogenase_complex "Branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex") (BCKDC), and the [glycine cleavage system](/wiki/Glycine_cleavage_system "Glycine cleavage system") (GCS), mtFAS significantly influences energy metabolism.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In contrast, longer-chain acyl-ACPs (C12–C18) [allosterically](/wiki/Allosteric_regulation "Allosteric regulation") activate the network of [LYRM proteins](/wiki/LYRM_protein "LYRM protein"), which comprises at least 12 members in humans and regulates mitochondrial [translation](/wiki/Translation_(biology) "Translation (biology)"), [iron-sulfur cluster](/wiki/Iron%E2%80%93sulfur_cluster "Iron–sulfur cluster") biogenesis, and the assembly of electron transport chain complexes.[[54]](#cite_note-Van_Vranken-2018-54)[[55]](#cite_note-Masud-2019-55) MtFAS and ACP thus coordinate the activation of mitochondrial respiration in response to substrate availability.[[54]](#cite_note-Van_Vranken-2018-54)[[56]](#cite_note-Nowinski-2020-56) This enables cells to increase their oxidative capacity when substrates are abundant and prevents the electron transport chain from running empty and inducing the formation of [reactive oxygen species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species") (ROS) under substrate-limited conditions.[[56]](#cite_note-Nowinski-2020-56)[[55]](#cite_note-Masud-2019-55)

MtFAS is also thought to play a role as a mediator in [intracellular signaling](/wiki/Cell_signaling "Cell signaling") due to its influence on the levels of bioactive lipids, such as [lysophospholipids](/wiki/Lysophospholipase "Lysophospholipase") and [sphingolipids](/wiki/Sphingolipid "Sphingolipid").[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Uptake, storage and release of calcium ions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Chondrocyte-_calcium_stain.jpg/500px-Chondrocyte-_calcium_stain.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chondrocyte-_calcium_stain.jpg)

[Transmission](/wiki/Transmission_electron_microscope "Transmission electron microscope") [electron micrograph](/wiki/Micrograph "Micrograph") of a [chondrocyte](/wiki/Chondrocyte "Chondrocyte"), stained for calcium, showing its nucleus (N) and mitochondria (M)

The concentrations of free calcium in the cell can regulate an array of reactions and is important for [signal transduction](/wiki/Signal_transduction "Signal transduction") in the cell. Mitochondria can transiently [store calcium](/wiki/Calcium_storage "Calcium storage"), a contributing process for the cell's homeostasis of calcium.[[58]](#cite_note-Santulli-2015c-58)
[[59]](#cite_note-Siegel-1999-59) Their ability to rapidly take in calcium for later release makes them good "cytosolic buffers" for calcium.[[60]](#cite_note-Rossier-2006-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62) The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is the most significant storage site of calcium,[[63]](#cite_note-Santulli-2015b-63) and there is a significant interplay between the mitochondrion and ER with regard to calcium.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The calcium is taken up into the [matrix](/wiki/Mitochondrial_matrix "Mitochondrial matrix") by the [mitochondrial calcium uniporter](/wiki/Mitochondrial_calcium_uniporter "Mitochondrial calcium uniporter") on the [inner mitochondrial membrane](/wiki/Inner_mitochondrial_membrane "Inner mitochondrial membrane").[[65]](#cite_note-Miller-1998-65) It is primarily driven by the mitochondrial [membrane potential](/wiki/Membrane_potential "Membrane potential").[[59]](#cite_note-Siegel-1999-59) Release of this calcium back into the cell's interior can occur via a sodium-calcium exchange protein or via "calcium-induced-calcium-release" pathways.[[65]](#cite_note-Miller-1998-65) This can initiate calcium spikes or calcium waves with large changes in the membrane potential. These can activate a series of [second messenger system](/wiki/Second_messenger_system "Second messenger system") proteins that can coordinate processes such as [neurotransmitter release](/wiki/Synaptic_vesicle "Synaptic vesicle") in nerve cells and release of [hormones](/wiki/Hormone "Hormone") in endocrine cells.[[66]](#cite_note-Santulli-2015a-66)

Ca2+ influx to the mitochondrial matrix has recently been implicated as a mechanism to regulate respiratory [bioenergetics](/wiki/Bioenergetics "Bioenergetics") by allowing the electrochemical potential across the membrane to transiently "pulse" from ΔΨ-dominated to pH-dominated, facilitating a reduction of [oxidative stress](/wiki/Oxidative_stress "Oxidative stress").[[67]](#cite_note-67) In neurons, concomitant increases in cytosolic and mitochondrial calcium act to synchronize neuronal activity with mitochondrial energy metabolism. Mitochondrial matrix calcium levels can reach the tens of micromolar levels, which is necessary for the activation of [isocitrate dehydrogenase](/wiki/Isocitrate_dehydrogenase "Isocitrate dehydrogenase"), one of the key regulatory enzymes of the [Krebs cycle](/wiki/Krebs_cycle "Krebs cycle").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Cellular proliferation regulation

The relationship between cellular proliferation and mitochondria has been investigated. Tumor cells require ample ATP to synthesize bioactive compounds such as [lipids](/wiki/Lipid "Lipid"), [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), and [nucleotides](/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide") for rapid proliferation.[[69]](#cite_note-Weinberg-2009-69) The majority of ATP in tumor cells is generated via the [oxidative phosphorylation](/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation "Oxidative phosphorylation") pathway (OxPhos).[[70]](#cite_note-Moreno-Sánchez-2007-70) Interference with OxPhos cause [cell cycle](/wiki/Cell_cycle "Cell cycle") arrest suggesting that mitochondria play a role in cell proliferation.[[70]](#cite_note-Moreno-Sánchez-2007-70) Mitochondrial ATP production is also vital for [cell division](/wiki/Cell_division "Cell division") and differentiation in infection[[71]](#cite_note-71) in addition to basic functions in the cell including the regulation of cell volume, solute [concentration](/wiki/Concentration "Concentration"), and cellular architecture.[[72]](#cite_note-Pedersen-1994-72)[[73]](#cite_note-Pattappa-2011-73)[[74]](#cite_note-Agarwal-2011-74) ATP levels differ at various stages of the cell cycle suggesting that there is a relationship between the abundance of ATP and the cell's ability to enter a new cell cycle.[[75]](#cite_note-Sweet-1999-75) ATP's role in the basic functions of the cell make the [cell cycle](/wiki/Cell_cycle "Cell cycle") sensitive to changes in the availability of mitochondrial derived ATP.[[75]](#cite_note-Sweet-1999-75) The variation in ATP levels at different stages of the cell cycle support the hypothesis that mitochondria play an important role in cell cycle regulation.[[75]](#cite_note-Sweet-1999-75) Although the specific mechanisms between mitochondria and the cell cycle regulation is not well understood, studies have shown that low energy cell cycle checkpoints monitor the energy capability before committing to another round of cell division.[[12]](#cite_note-McBride-2006-12)

### Programmed cell death and innate immunity

Further information: [Programmed cell death § Intrinsic Pathway](/wiki/Programmed_cell_death#Intrinsic_Pathway "Programmed cell death")

[Programmed cell death](/wiki/Programmed_cell_death "Programmed cell death") (PCD) is crucial for various physiological functions, including organ development and cellular homeostasis. It serves as an intrinsic mechanism to prevent [malignant transformation](/wiki/Malignant_transformation "Malignant transformation") and plays a fundamental role in [immunity](/wiki/Immune_system "Immune system") by aiding in antiviral defense, pathogen elimination, inflammation, and immune cell recruitment.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Mitochondria have long been recognized for their central role in the [intrinsic pathway](/wiki/Apoptosis#Intrinsic_pathway "Apoptosis") of [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis"), a form of PCD.[[77]](#cite_note-77) In recent decades, they have also been identified as a signalling hub for much of the [innate immune system](/wiki/Innate_immune_system "Innate immune system").[[78]](#cite_note-Bahat-2021-78) The [endosymbiotic origin](/wiki/Symbiogenesis "Symbiogenesis") of mitochondria distinguishes them from other cellular components, and the exposure of mitochondrial elements to the [cytosol](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol") can trigger the same pathways as infection markers. These pathways lead to [apoptosis](/wiki/Apoptosis "Apoptosis"), [autophagy](/wiki/Autophagy "Autophagy"), or the induction of proinflammatory genes.[[79]](#cite_note-Murphy-2024-79)[[78]](#cite_note-Bahat-2021-78)

Mitochondria contribute to apoptosis by releasing [cytochrome *c*](/wiki/Cytochrome_c "Cytochrome c"), which directly induces the formation of [apoptosomes](/wiki/Apoptosome "Apoptosome"). Additionally, they are a source of various [damage-associated molecular patterns](/wiki/Damage-associated_molecular_pattern "Damage-associated molecular pattern") (DAMPs). These DAMPs are often recognised by the same [pattern-recognition receptors](/wiki/Pattern_recognition_receptor "Pattern recognition receptor") (PRRs) that respond to [pathogen-associated molecular patterns](/wiki/Pathogen-associated_molecular_pattern "Pathogen-associated molecular pattern") (PAMPs) during infections.[[80]](#cite_note-80) For example, mitochondrial mtDNA resembles bacterial DNA due to its lack of [CpG](/wiki/CpG_site "CpG site") methylation and can be detected by [Toll-like receptor 9](/wiki/Toll-like_receptor_9 "Toll-like receptor 9") and [cGAS](/wiki/CGAS%E2%80%93STING_cytosolic_DNA_sensing_pathway "CGAS–STING cytosolic DNA sensing pathway").[[81]](#cite_note-81) [Double-stranded RNA](/wiki/DsRNA "DsRNA") (dsRNA), produced due to bidirectional mitochondrial transcription, can activate viral sensing pathways through [RIG-I-like receptors](/wiki/RIG-I-like_receptor "RIG-I-like receptor").[[82]](#cite_note-82) Additionally, the *N*-formylation of [mitochondrial proteins](/wiki/Human_mitochondrial_genetics "Human mitochondrial genetics"), similar to that of bacterial proteins, can be recognized by [formyl peptide receptors](/wiki/Formyl_peptide_receptor "Formyl peptide receptor").[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Normally, these mitochondrial components are sequestered from the rest of the cell but are released following mitochondrial membrane permeabilization during apoptosis or passively after mitochondrial damage. However, mitochondria also play an active role in innate immunity, releasing mtDNA in response to metabolic cues.[[78]](#cite_note-Bahat-2021-78) Mitochondria are also the [localization site](/wiki/Subcellular_localization "Subcellular localization") for immune and apoptosis regulatory proteins, such as [BAX](/wiki/Apoptosis_regulator_BAX "Apoptosis regulator BAX"), [MAVS](/wiki/Mitochondrial_antiviral-signaling_protein "Mitochondrial antiviral-signaling protein") (located on the [outer membrane](#Outer_membrane)), and [NLRX1](/wiki/NLRX1 "NLRX1") (found in the [matrix](#Matrix)). These proteins are modulated by the mitochondrial metabolic status and mitochondrial dynamics.[[78]](#cite_note-Bahat-2021-78)[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Donation

Some cells donate mitochondria to other cells. Such donations occur in multiple cell types, in organisms such as yeast, molluscs, and rodents. Mitochondrial donation was first observed in 2006. As of 2025,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Mitochondrion&action=edit) it had not been observed in humans *[in vivo](/wiki/In_vivo "In vivo")*. Donations may occur to help damaged cells, trigger tissue repair or the immune system, or to power distressed cells.[[87]](#cite_note-Conroy-2025-87)

Researchers cultured human mitochondria-free lung cancer cells with stem cells. The stem cells ejected mitochondria, which were absorbed by the lung cells. The lung cells then recovered their ability to divide and metabolize glucose. Mitochondria were then detected moving among lung, heart, brain, fat, bone, and other cells. Research has not identified how a cell indicates that it needs mitochondrial assistance or how other cells read those indicators.[[87]](#cite_note-Conroy-2025-87)

Various purposes have been observed to explain such donations. These include:[[87]](#cite_note-Conroy-2025-87)

* Restore function and extending lifespans of damaged cells[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)
* Endothelial cell donation to cancer cells can increase chemoresistance[[91]](#cite_note-91) or tumorigenic potential.[[92]](#cite_note-92)
* Following acute lung injury, stromal cells can donate mitochondria to lung cells, which in turn distributed ATP (fuel) to nearby cells that did not receive mitochondria.[[93]](#cite_note-93)
* Platelets can donate mitochondria to stem cells which then release molecules that aid in blood vessel formation, which accelerates wound healing. Bone cell donations had a similar effect.
* Maintain the blood-brain barrier
* Maintain macrophage function when their metabolism is disrupted
* Reduce inflammatory response, particularly when donated to T cells. Stem cells cultured from rheumatoid arthritis patients donated fewer mitochondria to T cells than do those from others.

Extracellular mitochondria use multiple modes of transport:[[87]](#cite_note-Conroy-2025-87)

* + tunnelling nanotubes that temporarily connect cells to transport various cargo[[94]](#cite_note-94)
  + passengers on [vesicles](/wiki/Vesicle_(biology_and_chemistry) "Vesicle (biology and chemistry)")
  + free-floating (typically in blood)
  + cell contact/fusion

### Additional functions

Mitochondria play a central role in many other [metabolic](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") tasks, such as:

* Signaling through mitochondrial [reactive oxygen species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species")[[95]](#cite_note-Li-2013-95)
* Regulation of the [membrane potential](/wiki/Membrane_potential "Membrane potential")[[21]](#cite_note-Voet-2006-21)
* Calcium signaling (including calcium-evoked apoptosis)[[96]](#cite_note-96)
* Regulation of cellular [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism")[[12]](#cite_note-McBride-2006-12)
* Certain [heme](/wiki/Heme "Heme") synthesis reactions[[97]](#cite_note-97) (see also: *[Porphyrin](/wiki/Porphyrin "Porphyrin")*)
* [Steroid](/wiki/Steroid "Steroid") synthesis[[60]](#cite_note-Rossier-2006-60)
* Hormonal signaling[[98]](#cite_note-98) – mitochondria are sensitive and responsive to hormones, in part by the action of mitochondrial estrogen receptors (mtERs). These receptors have been found in various tissues and cell types, including brain[[99]](#cite_note-99) and heart[[100]](#cite_note-100)
* Development and function of immune cells[[101]](#cite_note-101)
* Neuronal mitochondria also contribute to cellular quality control by reporting neuronal status towards microglia through specialised somatic-junctions.[[102]](#cite_note-Cserép-2020-102)
* Mitochondria of developing neurons contribute to intercellular signaling towards [microglia](/wiki/Microglia "Microglia"), which communication is indispensable for proper regulation of brain development.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

Some mitochondrial functions are performed only in specific types of cells. For example, mitochondria in [liver cells](/wiki/Liver_cell "Liver cell") contain enzymes that allow them to detoxify [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia"), a waste product of protein metabolism. A mutation in the genes regulating any of these functions can result in [mitochondrial diseases](/wiki/Mitochondrial_disease "Mitochondrial disease").

Mitochondrial proteins (proteins transcribed from mitochondrial DNA) vary depending on the tissue and the species. In humans, 615 distinct types of proteins have been identified from [cardiac](/wiki/Heart "Heart") mitochondria,[[104]](#cite_note-104) whereas in [rats](/wiki/Murinae "Murinae"), 940 proteins have been reported.[[105]](#cite_note-105) The mitochondrial [proteome](/wiki/Proteome "Proteome") is thought to be dynamically regulated.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

## Organization and distribution

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/HeLa_mtGFP.tif/lossy-page1-250px-HeLa_mtGFP.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:HeLa_mtGFP.tif)

Typical mitochondrial network (green) in two human cells ([HeLa cells](/wiki/HeLa_cells "HeLa cells"))

Mitochondria (or related structures) are found in all [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") (except the [Oxymonad](/wiki/Oxymonad "Oxymonad") *[Monocercomonoides](/wiki/Monocercomonoides "Monocercomonoides")*).[[5]](#cite_note-Karnkowska-2016-5) Although commonly depicted as bean-like structures they form a highly dynamic network in the majority of cells where they constantly undergo [fission](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fission "Mitochondrial fission") and [fusion](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fusion "Mitochondrial fusion"). The population of all the mitochondria of a given cell constitutes the **chondriome**.[[107]](#cite_note-Logan-2010-107) Mitochondria vary in number and location according to cell type. A single mitochondrion is often found in unicellular organisms, while human liver cells have about 1000–2000 mitochondria per cell, making up 1/5 of the cell volume.[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20) The mitochondrial content of otherwise similar cells can vary substantially in size and membrane potential,[[108]](#cite_note-108) with differences arising from sources including uneven partitioning at cell division, leading to [extrinsic differences](/wiki/Cellular_noise "Cellular noise") in ATP levels and downstream cellular processes.[[109]](#cite_note-109) The mitochondria can be found nestled between [myofibrils](/wiki/Myofibril "Myofibril") of [muscle](/wiki/Muscle "Muscle") or wrapped around the [sperm](/wiki/Sperm "Sperm") [flagellum](/wiki/Flagellum "Flagellum").[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20) Often, they form a complex 3D branching network inside the cell with the [cytoskeleton](/wiki/Cytoskeleton "Cytoskeleton"). The association with the cytoskeleton determines mitochondrial shape, which can affect the function as well:[[110]](#cite_note-110) different structures of the mitochondrial network may afford the population a variety of physical, chemical, and signalling advantages or disadvantages.[[111]](#cite_note-111) Mitochondria in cells are always distributed along microtubules and the distribution of these organelles is also correlated with the [endoplasmic reticulum](/wiki/Endoplasmic_reticulum "Endoplasmic reticulum").[[112]](#cite_note-112) Recent evidence suggests that [vimentin](/wiki/Vimentin "Vimentin"), one of the components of the cytoskeleton, is also critical to the association with the cytoskeleton.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

### Mitochondria-associated ER membrane (MAM)

Main article: [Mitochondria associated membranes](/wiki/Mitochondria_associated_membranes "Mitochondria associated membranes")

The mitochondria-associated ER membrane (MAM) is another structural element that is increasingly recognized for its critical role in cellular physiology and [homeostasis](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis"). Once considered a technical snag in cell fractionation techniques, the alleged ER vesicle contaminants that invariably appeared in the mitochondrial fraction have been re-identified as membranous structures derived from the MAM—the interface between mitochondria and the ER.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114) Physical coupling between these two organelles had previously been observed in electron micrographs and has more recently been probed with [fluorescence microscopy](/wiki/Fluorescence_microscopy "Fluorescence microscopy").[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114) Such studies estimate that at the MAM, which may comprise up to 20% of the mitochondrial outer membrane, the ER and mitochondria are separated by a mere 10–25 nm and held together by protein tethering complexes.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114)[[30]](#cite_note-Hayashi-2009-30)[[115]](#cite_note-de_Brito-2010-115)

Purified MAM from subcellular fractionation is enriched in enzymes involved in phospholipid exchange, in addition to channels associated with Ca2+ signaling.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114)[[115]](#cite_note-de_Brito-2010-115) These hints of a prominent role for the MAM in the regulation of cellular lipid stores and signal transduction have been borne out, with significant implications for mitochondrial-associated cellular phenomena, as discussed below. Not only has the MAM provided insight into the mechanistic basis underlying such physiological processes as intrinsic apoptosis and the propagation of calcium signaling, but it also favors a more refined view of the mitochondria. Though often seen as static, isolated 'powerhouses' hijacked for cellular metabolism through an ancient endosymbiotic event, the evolution of the MAM underscores the extent to which mitochondria have been integrated into overall cellular physiology, with intimate physical and functional coupling to the endomembrane system.

#### Phospholipid transfer

The MAM is enriched in enzymes involved in lipid biosynthesis, such as phosphatidylserine synthase on the ER face and phosphatidylserine decarboxylase on the mitochondrial face.[[116]](#cite_note-Vance-1996-116)[[117]](#cite_note-Lebiedzinska-2009-117) Because mitochondria are dynamic organelles constantly undergoing [fission](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fission "Mitochondrial fission") and [fusion](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fusion "Mitochondrial fusion") events, they require a constant and well-regulated supply of phospholipids for membrane integrity.[[118]](#cite_note-Twig-2008-118)[[119]](#cite_note-Osman-2011-119) But mitochondria are not only a destination for the phospholipids they finish synthesis of; rather, this organelle also plays a role in inter-organelle trafficking of the intermediates and products of phospholipid biosynthetic pathways, ceramide and cholesterol metabolism, and glycosphingolipid anabolism.[[117]](#cite_note-Lebiedzinska-2009-117)[[119]](#cite_note-Osman-2011-119)

Such trafficking capacity depends on the MAM, which has been shown to facilitate transfer of lipid intermediates between organelles.[[116]](#cite_note-Vance-1996-116) In contrast to the standard vesicular mechanism of lipid transfer, evidence indicates that the physical proximity of the ER and mitochondrial membranes at the MAM allows for lipid flipping between opposed bilayers.[[119]](#cite_note-Osman-2011-119) Despite this unusual and seemingly energetically unfavorable mechanism, such transport does not require ATP.[[119]](#cite_note-Osman-2011-119) Instead, in yeast, it has been shown to be dependent on a [multiprotein](/wiki/Protein_complex "Protein complex") tethering structure termed the ER-mitochondria encounter structure, or ERMES, although it remains unclear whether this structure directly mediates lipid transfer or is required to keep the membranes in sufficiently close proximity to lower the [energy barrier](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") for [lipid](/wiki/Lipid "Lipid") flipping.[[119]](#cite_note-Osman-2011-119)[[120]](#cite_note-Kornmann-2009-120)

The MAM may also be part of the secretory pathway, in addition to its role in intracellular lipid trafficking. In particular, the MAM appears to be an intermediate destination between the rough ER and the Golgi in the pathway that leads to [very-low-density lipoprotein](/wiki/Very-low-density_lipoprotein "Very-low-density lipoprotein"), or VLDL, assembly and secretion.[[117]](#cite_note-Lebiedzinska-2009-117)[[121]](#cite_note-Rusiñol-1994-121) The MAM thus serves as a critical metabolic and trafficking hub in lipid metabolism.

#### Calcium signaling

A critical role for the ER in calcium signaling was acknowledged before such a role for the mitochondria was widely accepted, in part because the low affinity of Ca2+ channels localized to the outer mitochondrial membrane seemed to contradict this organelle's purported responsiveness to changes in intracellular Ca2+ flux.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114)[[63]](#cite_note-Santulli-2015b-63) But the presence of the MAM resolves this apparent contradiction: the close physical association between the two organelles results in Ca2+ microdomains at contact points that facilitate efficient Ca2+ transmission from the ER to the mitochondria.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114) Transmission occurs in response to so-called "Ca2+ puffs" generated by spontaneous clustering and activation of [IP3R](/wiki/IP3R "IP3R"), a canonical ER membrane Ca2+ channel.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114)[[30]](#cite_note-Hayashi-2009-30)

The fate of these puffs—in particular, whether they remain restricted to isolated locales or integrated into Ca2+ waves for propagation throughout the cell—is determined in large part by MAM dynamics. Although reuptake of Ca2+ by the ER (concomitant with its release) modulates the intensity of the puffs, thus insulating mitochondria to a certain degree from high Ca2+ exposure, the MAM often serves as a firewall that essentially buffers Ca2+ puffs by acting as a sink into which free ions released into the cytosol can be funneled.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114)[[122]](#cite_note-Kopach-2008-122)[[123]](#cite_note-Csordás-2001-123) This Ca2+ tunneling occurs through the low-affinity Ca2+ receptor [VDAC1](/wiki/VDAC1 "VDAC1"), which recently has been shown to be physically [tethered](/wiki/Tether_(cell_biology) "Tether (cell biology)") to the IP3R clusters on the ER membrane and enriched at the MAM.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114)[[30]](#cite_note-Hayashi-2009-30)[[124]](#cite_note-Decuypere-2011-124) The ability of mitochondria to serve as a Ca2+ sink is a result of the electrochemical gradient generated during oxidative phosphorylation, which makes tunneling of the cation an exergonic process.[[124]](#cite_note-Decuypere-2011-124) Normal, mild calcium influx from cytosol into the mitochondrial matrix causes transient depolarization that is corrected by pumping out protons.

But transmission of Ca2+ is not unidirectional; rather, it is a two-way street.[[63]](#cite_note-Santulli-2015b-63) The properties of the Ca2+ pump SERCA and the channel IP3R present on the ER membrane facilitate feedback regulation coordinated by MAM function. In particular, the clearance of Ca2+ by the MAM allows for [spatio-temporal patterning](/wiki/Spatio-temporal_pattern "Spatio-temporal pattern") of Ca2+ signaling because Ca2+ alters IP3R activity in a biphasic manner.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114) [SERCA](/wiki/SERCA "SERCA") is likewise affected by mitochondrial feedback: uptake of Ca2+ by the MAM stimulates ATP production, thus providing energy that enables SERCA to reload the ER with Ca2+ for continued Ca2+ efflux at the MAM.[[122]](#cite_note-Kopach-2008-122)[[124]](#cite_note-Decuypere-2011-124) Thus, the MAM is not a passive buffer for Ca2+ puffs; rather it helps modulate further Ca2+ signaling through feedback loops that affect ER dynamics.

Regulating ER release of Ca2+ at the MAM is especially critical because only a certain window of Ca2+ uptake sustains the mitochondria, and consequently the cell, at homeostasis. Sufficient intraorganelle Ca2+ signaling is required to stimulate metabolism by activating dehydrogenase enzymes critical to flux through the citric acid cycle.[[125]](#cite_note-Diercks-2017-125)[[126]](#cite_note-Hajnóczky-2011-126) However, once Ca2+ signaling in the mitochondria passes a certain threshold, it stimulates the intrinsic pathway of apoptosis in part by collapsing the mitochondrial membrane potential required for metabolism.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114) Studies examining the role of pro- and anti-apoptotic factors support this model; for example, the anti-apoptotic factor Bcl-2 has been shown to interact with IP3Rs to reduce Ca2+ filling of the ER, leading to reduced efflux at the MAM and preventing collapse of the mitochondrial membrane potential post-apoptotic stimuli.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114) Given the need for such fine regulation of Ca2+ signaling, it is perhaps unsurprising that dysregulated mitochondrial Ca2+ has been implicated in several neurodegenerative diseases, while the catalogue of tumor suppressors includes a few that are enriched at the MAM.[[124]](#cite_note-Decuypere-2011-124)

#### Molecular basis for tethering

Recent advances in the identification of the [tethers](/wiki/Tether_(cell_biology) "Tether (cell biology)") between the mitochondrial and ER membranes suggest that the scaffolding function of the molecular elements involved is secondary to other, non-structural functions. In yeast, ERMES, a multiprotein complex of interacting ER- and mitochondrial-resident membrane proteins, is required for lipid transfer at the MAM and exemplifies this principle. One of its components, for example, is also a constituent of the protein complex required for insertion of transmembrane beta-barrel proteins into the lipid bilayer.[[119]](#cite_note-Osman-2011-119) However, a [homologue](/wiki/Homology_(biology) "Homology (biology)") of the ERMES complex has not yet been identified in mammalian cells. Other proteins implicated in scaffolding likewise have functions independent of structural tethering at the MAM; for example, ER-resident and mitochondrial-resident mitofusins form heterocomplexes that regulate the number of inter-organelle contact sites, although mitofusins were first identified for their role in [fission](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fission "Mitochondrial fission") and [fusion](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fusion "Mitochondrial fusion") events between individual mitochondria.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114) [Glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose")-related protein 75 (grp75) is another dual-function protein. In addition to the matrix pool of grp75, a portion serves as a chaperone that physically links the mitochondrial and ER Ca2+ channels VDAC and IP3R for efficient Ca2+ transmission at the MAM.[[114]](#cite_note-Rizzuto-2009-114)[[30]](#cite_note-Hayashi-2009-30) Another potential tether is [Sigma-1R](/wiki/Sigma-1_receptor "Sigma-1 receptor"), a non-opioid receptor whose stabilization of ER-resident IP3R may preserve communication at the MAM during the metabolic stress response.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128)

[![ERMES tethering complex.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/ba/ERMES.png)](/wiki/File:ERMES.png)

Model of the yeast multimeric tethering complex, ERMES

#### Perspective

The MAM is a critical signaling, metabolic, and trafficking hub in the cell that allows for the integration of ER and mitochondrial physiology. Coupling between these organelles is not simply structural but functional as well and critical for overall cellular physiology and [homeostasis](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis"). The MAM thus offers a perspective on mitochondria that diverges from the traditional view of this organelle as a static, isolated unit appropriated for its metabolic capacity by the cell.[[129]](#cite_note-Csordás-2018-129) Instead, this mitochondrial-ER interface emphasizes the integration of the mitochondria, the product of an endosymbiotic event, into diverse cellular processes. Recently it has also been shown, that mitochondria and MAM-s in neurons are anchored to specialised intercellular communication sites (so called somatic-junctions). [Microglial](/wiki/Microglia "Microglia") processes monitor and protect neuronal functions at these sites, and MAM-s are supposed to have an important role in this type of cellular quality-control.[[102]](#cite_note-Cserép-2020-102)

## Origin and evolution

Main article: [Symbiogenesis](/wiki/Symbiogenesis "Symbiogenesis")

There are two hypotheses about the origin of mitochondria: [endosymbiotic](/wiki/Endosymbiotic_theory "Endosymbiotic theory") and [autogenous](/wiki/Autotransplantation "Autotransplantation"). The endosymbiotic hypothesis suggests that mitochondria were originally [prokaryotic](/wiki/Prokaryote "Prokaryote") cells, capable of implementing oxidative mechanisms that were not possible for eukaryotic cells; they became [endosymbionts](/wiki/Endosymbiont "Endosymbiont") living inside the eukaryote.[[23]](#cite_note-Gabaldón-2021-23)[[130]](#cite_note-McCutcheon-2021-130)[[131]](#cite_note-Callier-2022-131)[[132]](#cite_note-Margulis-1986-132) In the autogenous hypothesis, mitochondria were born by splitting off a portion of DNA from the nucleus of the eukaryotic cell at the time of divergence with the prokaryotes; this DNA portion would have been enclosed by membranes, which could not be crossed by proteins. Since mitochondria have many features in common with [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria"), the endosymbiotic hypothesis is the more widely accepted of the two accounts.[[132]](#cite_note-Margulis-1986-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133)

A [mitochondrion contains DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA"), which is organized as several copies of a single, usually [circular](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA#Circular_versus_linear "Mitochondrial DNA") [chromosome](/wiki/Chromosome "Chromosome"). This mitochondrial chromosome contains genes for [redox](/wiki/Redox "Redox") proteins, such as those of the respiratory chain. The [CoRR hypothesis](/wiki/CoRR_hypothesis "CoRR hypothesis") proposes that this co-location is required for redox regulation. The mitochondrial [genome](/wiki/Genome "Genome") codes for some [RNAs](/wiki/RNA "RNA") of [ribosomes](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome"), and the 22 [tRNAs](/wiki/TRNA "TRNA") necessary for the translation of [mRNAs](/wiki/MRNA "MRNA") into protein. The circular structure is also found in prokaryotes. The [proto-mitochondrion](/wiki/Proto-mitochondrion "Proto-mitochondrion") was probably closely related to the order *[Rickettsiales](/wiki/Rickettsiales "Rickettsiales")*, which is in class Alphaproteobacteria of phylum Pseudomonadota.[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) However, the exact relationship of the ancestor of mitochondria to the [alphaproteobacteria](/wiki/Alphaproteobacteria "Alphaproteobacteria") and whether the mitochondrion was formed at the same time or after the nucleus, remains controversial.[[136]](#cite_note-136) For example, it has been suggested that the [SAR11 clade](/wiki/SAR11_clade "SAR11 clade") of bacteria shares a relatively recent common ancestor with the mitochondria,[[137]](#cite_note-137) while [phylogenomic](/wiki/Phylogenomic "Phylogenomic") analyses indicate that mitochondria evolved from a [Pseudomonadota](/wiki/Pseudomonadota "Pseudomonadota") lineage that is closely related to or a member of [alphaproteobacteria](/wiki/Alphaproteobacteria "Alphaproteobacteria").[[138]](#cite_note-Martijn-2018-138)[[139]](#cite_note-139) Some papers describe mitochondria as sister to the alphaproteobacteria, together forming the sister to the marineproteo1 group, together forming the sister to [Magnetococcidae](/wiki/Magnetococcidae "Magnetococcidae").[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143)

|  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Pseudomonadota](/wiki/Pseudomonadota "Pseudomonadota") | |  |  | | --- | --- | |  |  | |  | | [Alphaproteobacteria s.l.](/wiki/Alphaproteobacteria "Alphaproteobacteria") | |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | [Magnetococcales](/wiki/Magnetococcales "Magnetococcales") | |  | |  | |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | [MarineProteo1](/w/index.php?title=MarineProteo1&action=edit&redlink=1 "MarineProteo1 (page does not exist)") | |  | |  | |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | **Mitochondria** | |  | |  | [Alphaproteobacteria s.s.](/wiki/Alphaproteobacteria "Alphaproteobacteria") | |  | | |  | | |  | | |  | |
|  |

The ribosomes coded for by the mitochondrial DNA are similar to those from bacteria in size and structure.[[144]](#cite_note-O'Brien-2003-144) They closely resemble the bacterial [70S](/wiki/Ribosome#Structure "Ribosome") ribosome and not the [80S](/wiki/Ribosome#Structure "Ribosome") [cytoplasmic](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm") ribosomes, which are coded for by [nuclear](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus") DNA.

The [endosymbiotic](/wiki/Endosymbiotic "Endosymbiotic") relationship of mitochondria with their host cells was popularized by [Lynn Margulis](/wiki/Lynn_Margulis "Lynn Margulis").[[145]](#cite_note-145) The [endosymbiotic theory](/wiki/Symbiogenesis "Symbiogenesis") suggests that mitochondria descended from aerobic bacteria that somehow survived [endocytosis](/wiki/Endocytosis "Endocytosis") by another cell, and became incorporated into the [cytoplasm](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm"). The ability of these bacteria to conduct [respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration") in host cells that had relied on [glycolysis](/wiki/Glycolysis "Glycolysis") and [fermentation](/wiki/Fermentation_(biochemistry) "Fermentation (biochemistry)") would have provided a considerable evolutionary advantage. This symbiotic relationship probably developed 1.7 to 2 billion years ago.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/The-origins-of-mitochondrion-related-organelles-A-hypothetical-scenario-for-the.png/250px-The-origins-of-mitochondrion-related-organelles-A-hypothetical-scenario-for-the.png)](/wiki/File:The-origins-of-mitochondrion-related-organelles-A-hypothetical-scenario-for-the.png)

Evolution of mitochondrion related organelles (MROs)

A few groups of unicellular eukaryotes have only vestigial mitochondria or derived structures: the [microsporidians](/wiki/Microsporidia "Microsporidia"), [metamonads](/wiki/Metamonad "Metamonad"), and [archamoebae](/wiki/Archamoebae "Archamoebae").[[148]](#cite_note-Cavalier-Smith-1991-148) These groups appear as the most primitive eukaryotes on [phylogenetic trees](/wiki/Phylogenetic_trees "Phylogenetic trees") constructed using [rRNA](/wiki/RRNA "RRNA") information, which once suggested that they appeared before the origin of mitochondria. However, this is now known to be an artifact of *[long-branch attraction](/wiki/Long-branch_attraction "Long-branch attraction")*: They are derived groups and retain genes or organelles derived from mitochondria (e. g., [mitosomes](/wiki/Mitosome "Mitosome") and [hydrogenosomes](/wiki/Hydrogenosome "Hydrogenosome")).[[8]](#cite_note-Henze-2003-8) Hydrogenosomes, mitosomes, and related organelles as found in some [loricifera](/wiki/Loricifera "Loricifera") (e. g. *[Spinoloricus](/wiki/Spinoloricus "Spinoloricus")*)[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150) and [myxozoa](/wiki/Myxozoa "Myxozoa") (e. g. *[Henneguya zschokkei](/wiki/Henneguya_zschokkei "Henneguya zschokkei")*) are together classified as MROs, mitochondrion-related organelles.[[7]](#cite_note-Yahalomi-2020-7)[[151]](#cite_note-Shiflett-2010-151)

*[Monocercomonoides](/wiki/Monocercomonoides "Monocercomonoides")* and other [oxymonads](/wiki/Oxymonad "Oxymonad") appear to have lost their mitochondria completely and at least some of the mitochondrial functions seem to be carried out by cytoplasmic proteins now.[[5]](#cite_note-Karnkowska-2016-5)[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[10]](#cite_note-Novák-2023-10)

## Mitochondrial genetics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Map_of_the_human_mitochondrial_genome.svg/330px-Map_of_the_human_mitochondrial_genome.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_human_mitochondrial_genome.svg)

The [circular](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA#Circular_versus_linear "Mitochondrial DNA") 16,569 bp human mitochondrial genome encoding 37 genes, *i.e.*, 28 on the H-strand and 9 on the L-strand

Main article: [Mitochondrial DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA")

Mitochondria contain their own genome. The [human mitochondrial genome](/wiki/Human_mitochondrial_genetics "Human mitochondrial genetics") is a circular double-stranded [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") molecule of about 16 [kilobases](/wiki/Base_pair "Base pair").[[153]](#cite_note-Chan-2006-153) It encodes 37 genes: 13 for [subunits](/wiki/Protein_subunit "Protein subunit") of respiratory complexes I, III, IV and V, 22 for mitochondrial [tRNA](/wiki/TRNA "TRNA") (for the 20 standard amino acids, plus an extra gene for leucine and serine), and 2 for [rRNA](/wiki/RRNA "RRNA") (12S and 16S rRNA).[[153]](#cite_note-Chan-2006-153) One mitochondrion can contain two to ten copies of its DNA.[[154]](#cite_note-Wiesner-1992-154) One of the two mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) strands has a disproportionately higher ratio of the heavier nucleotides adenine and guanine, and this is termed the heavy strand (or H strand), whereas the other strand is termed the light strand (or L strand). The weight difference allows the two strands to be separated by [centrifugation](/wiki/Centrifugation "Centrifugation"). mtDNA has one long non-coding stretch known as the non-coding region (NCR), which contains the heavy strand promoter (HSP) and light strand promoter (LSP) for RNA transcription, the origin of replication for the H strand (OriH) localized on the L strand, three conserved sequence boxes (CSBs 1–3), and a termination-associated sequence (TAS). The origin of replication for the L strand (OriL) is localized on the H strand 11,000 bp downstream of OriH, located within a cluster of genes coding for tRNA.[[155]](#cite_note-155)

As in prokaryotes, there is a very high proportion of coding DNA and an absence of repeats. Mitochondrial genes are [transcribed](/wiki/Transcription_(genetics) "Transcription (genetics)") as multigenic transcripts, which are cleaved and [polyadenylated](/wiki/Polyadenylation "Polyadenylation") to yield mature [mRNAs](/wiki/MRNA "MRNA"). Most proteins necessary for mitochondrial function are encoded by genes in the [cell nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus") and the corresponding proteins are imported into the mitochondrion.[[156]](#cite_note-Anderson-1981-156) The exact number of genes encoded by the nucleus and the [mitochondrial genome](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") differs between species. Most mitochondrial genomes are circular.[[157]](#cite_note-Fukuhara-1993-157) In general, mitochondrial DNA lacks [introns](/wiki/Intron "Intron"), as is the case in the human mitochondrial genome;[[156]](#cite_note-Anderson-1981-156) however, introns have been observed in some eukaryotic mitochondrial DNA,[[158]](#cite_note-158) such as that of [yeast](/wiki/Yeast "Yeast")[[159]](#cite_note-159) and [protists](/wiki/Protist "Protist"),[[160]](#cite_note-160) including *[Dictyostelium](/wiki/Dictyostelium "Dictyostelium") discoideum*.[[161]](#cite_note-161) Between protein-coding regions, tRNAs are present. Mitochondrial tRNA genes have different sequences from the nuclear tRNAs, but lookalikes of mitochondrial tRNAs have been found in the nuclear chromosomes with high sequence similarity.[[162]](#cite_note-Telonis-2014-162)

In animals, the mitochondrial genome is typically a single circular chromosome that is approximately 16 kb long and has 37 genes. The genes, while highly conserved, may vary in location. Curiously, this pattern is not found in the human body louse (*[Pediculus humanus](/wiki/Pediculus_humanus "Pediculus humanus")*). Instead, this mitochondrial genome is arranged in 18 minicircular chromosomes, each of which is 3–4 kb long and has one to three genes.[[163]](#cite_note-Shao-2009-163) This pattern is also found in other [sucking lice](/wiki/Anoplura "Anoplura"), but not in [chewing lice](/wiki/Mallophaga "Mallophaga"). Recombination has been shown to occur between the minichromosomes.

### Human population genetic studies

Main article: [Human mitochondrial genetics](/wiki/Human_mitochondrial_genetics "Human mitochondrial genetics")

The near-absence of [genetic recombination](/wiki/Genetic_recombination "Genetic recombination") in mitochondrial DNA makes it a useful source of information for studying [population genetics](/wiki/Population_genetics "Population genetics") and [evolutionary biology](/wiki/Evolutionary_biology "Evolutionary biology").[[164]](#cite_note-164) Because all the mitochondrial DNA is inherited as a single unit, or [haplotype](/wiki/Haplotype "Haplotype"), the relationships between mitochondrial DNA from different individuals can be represented as a [gene tree](/wiki/Phylogenetic_tree "Phylogenetic tree"). Patterns in these gene trees can be used to infer the evolutionary history of populations. The classic example of this is in [human evolutionary genetics](/wiki/Human_evolutionary_genetics "Human evolutionary genetics"), where the [molecular clock](/wiki/Molecular_clock "Molecular clock") can be used to provide a recent date for [mitochondrial Eve](/wiki/Mitochondrial_Eve "Mitochondrial Eve").[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166) This is often interpreted as strong support for a recent modern human expansion [out of Africa](/wiki/Recent_single-origin_hypothesis "Recent single-origin hypothesis").[[167]](#cite_note-Garrigan-2006-167) Another human example is the sequencing of mitochondrial DNA from [Neanderthal](/wiki/Neanderthal "Neanderthal") bones. The relatively large evolutionary distance between the mitochondrial DNA sequences of Neanderthals and living humans has been interpreted as evidence for the lack of interbreeding between Neanderthals and modern humans.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

However, mitochondrial DNA reflects only the history of the females in a population. This can be partially overcome by the use of paternal genetic sequences, such as the [non-recombining](/wiki/Genetic_recombination "Genetic recombination") region of the [Y-chromosome](/wiki/Y-chromosome "Y-chromosome").[[167]](#cite_note-Garrigan-2006-167)

Recent measurements of the [molecular clock](/wiki/Molecular_clock "Molecular clock") for mitochondrial DNA[[169]](#cite_note-169) reported a value of 1 mutation every 7884 years dating back to the most recent common ancestor of humans and apes, which is consistent with estimates of mutation rates of autosomal DNA (10−8 per base per generation).[[170]](#cite_note-Nachman-2000-170)

### Alternative genetic code

Exceptions to the standard genetic code in mitochondria[[20]](#cite_note-Alberts-2005-20)

| Organism | Codon | Standard | Mitochondria |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") | AGA, AGG | Arginine | Stop codon |
| [Invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") | AGA, AGG | Arginine | Serine |
| [Fungi](/wiki/Fungus "Fungus") | CUA | Leucine | Threonine |
| All of the above | AUA | Isoleucine | Methionine |
| UGA | Stop codon | Tryptophan |

While slight variations on the standard [genetic code](/wiki/Genetic_code "Genetic code") had been predicted earlier,[[171]](#cite_note-171) none was discovered until 1979, when researchers studying [human mitochondrial genes](/wiki/Human_mitochondrial_genetics "Human mitochondrial genetics") determined that they used an alternative code.[[172]](#cite_note-172) Nonetheless, the mitochondria of many other eukaryotes, including most plants, use the standard code.[[173]](#cite_note-Elzanowski-2019-173) Many slight variants have been discovered since,[[173]](#cite_note-Elzanowski-2019-173) including various alternative mitochondrial codes.[[174]](#cite_note-174) Further, the AUA, AUC, and AUU codons are all allowable start codons.

Some of these differences should be regarded as pseudo-changes in the genetic code due to the phenomenon of [RNA editing](/wiki/RNA_editing "RNA editing"), which is common in mitochondria. In higher plants, it was thought that CGG encoded for [tryptophan](/wiki/Tryptophan "Tryptophan") and not [arginine](/wiki/Arginine "Arginine"); however, the codon in the processed RNA was discovered to be the UGG codon, consistent with the standard [genetic code](/wiki/Genetic_code "Genetic code") for tryptophan.[[175]](#cite_note-175) Of note, the arthropod mitochondrial genetic code has undergone parallel evolution within a phylum, with some organisms uniquely translating AGG to lysine.[[176]](#cite_note-176)

### Replication and inheritance

Main article: [Mitochondrial fission](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fission "Mitochondrial fission")
