Mitochondria divide by [mitochondrial fission](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fission "Mitochondrial fission"), a form of [binary fission](/wiki/Binary_fission "Binary fission") that is also done by bacteria[[177]](#cite_note-177) although the process is tightly regulated by the host eukaryotic cell and involves communication between and contact with several other organelles. The regulation of this division differs between eukaryotes. In many single-celled eukaryotes, their growth and division are linked to the [cell cycle](/wiki/Cell_cycle "Cell cycle"). For example, a single mitochondrion may divide synchronously with the nucleus. This division and segregation process must be tightly controlled so that each daughter cell receives at least one mitochondrion. In other eukaryotes (in mammals for example), mitochondria may replicate their DNA and divide mainly in response to the energy needs of the cell, rather than in phase with the cell cycle. When the energy needs of a cell are high, mitochondria grow and divide. When energy use is low, mitochondria are destroyed or become inactive. In such examples mitochondria are apparently randomly distributed to the daughter cells during the division of the [cytoplasm](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm"). Mitochondrial dynamics, the balance between [mitochondrial fusion](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fusion "Mitochondrial fusion") and [fission](/wiki/Mitochondrial_fission "Mitochondrial fission"), is an important factor in pathologies associated with several disease conditions.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

The hypothesis of mitochondrial binary fission has relied on the visualization by fluorescence microscopy and conventional [transmission electron microscopy](/wiki/Transmission_electron_microscopy "Transmission electron microscopy") (TEM). The resolution of fluorescence microscopy (≈200 nm) is insufficient to distinguish structural details, such as double mitochondrial membrane in mitochondrial division or even to distinguish individual mitochondria when several are close together. Conventional TEM has also some technical limitations[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] in verifying mitochondrial division. [Cryo-electron tomography](/wiki/Cryo-electron_tomography "Cryo-electron tomography") was recently used to visualize mitochondrial division in frozen hydrated intact cells. It revealed that mitochondria divide by budding.[[179]](#cite_note-179)

An individual's mitochondrial genes are inherited only from the mother, with rare exceptions.[[180]](#cite_note-McWilliams-2019-180) In humans, when an [egg cell](/wiki/Ovum "Ovum") is fertilized by a sperm, the mitochondria, and therefore the mitochondrial DNA, usually come from the egg only. The sperm's mitochondria enter the egg, but do not contribute genetic information to the embryo.[[181]](#cite_note-181) Instead, paternal mitochondria are marked with [ubiquitin](/wiki/Ubiquitin "Ubiquitin") to select them for later destruction inside the [embryo](/wiki/Embryo "Embryo").[[182]](#cite_note-182) The egg cell contains relatively few mitochondria, but these mitochondria divide to populate the cells of the adult organism. This mode is seen in most organisms, including the majority of animals. However, mitochondria in some species can sometimes be inherited paternally. This is the norm among certain [coniferous](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") plants, although not in [pine trees](/wiki/Pine_tree "Pine tree") and [yews](/wiki/Taxus "Taxus").[[183]](#cite_note-183) For [Mytilids](/wiki/Mytilidae "Mytilidae"), paternal inheritance only occurs within males of the species.[[184]](#cite_note-184)[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186) It has been suggested that it occurs at a very low level in humans.[[187]](#cite_note-187)

[Uniparental inheritance](/wiki/Uniparental_inheritance "Uniparental inheritance") leads to little opportunity for [genetic recombination](/wiki/Genetic_recombination "Genetic recombination") between different lineages of mitochondria, although a single mitochondrion can contain 2–10 copies of its DNA.[[154]](#cite_note-Wiesner-1992-154) What recombination does take place maintains genetic integrity rather than maintaining diversity. However, there are studies showing evidence of recombination in mitochondrial DNA. It is clear that the enzymes necessary for recombination are present in mammalian cells.[[188]](#cite_note-188) Further, evidence suggests that animal mitochondria can undergo recombination.[[189]](#cite_note-189) The data are more controversial in humans, although indirect evidence of recombination exists.[[190]](#cite_note-190)[[191]](#cite_note-191)

Entities undergoing uniparental inheritance and with little to no recombination may be expected to be subject to [Muller's ratchet](/wiki/Muller%27s_ratchet "Muller's ratchet"), the accumulation of deleterious mutations until functionality is lost. Animal populations of mitochondria avoid this buildup through a developmental process known as the [mtDNA bottleneck](/wiki/Heteroplasmy#Mitochondrial_bottleneck "Heteroplasmy"). The bottleneck exploits [stochastic processes in the cell](/wiki/Cellular_noise "Cellular noise") to increase the cell-to-cell variability in [mutant load](/wiki/Heteroplasmy "Heteroplasmy") as an organism develops: a single egg cell with some proportion of mutant mtDNA thus produces an embryo where different cells have different mutant loads. Cell-level selection may then act to remove those cells with more mutant mtDNA, leading to a stabilization or reduction in mutant load between generations. The mechanism underlying the bottleneck is debated,[[192]](#cite_note-192)[[193]](#cite_note-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194) with a recent mathematical and experimental metastudy providing evidence for a combination of random partitioning of mtDNAs at cell divisions and random turnover of mtDNA molecules within the cell.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

### DNA repair

Mitochondria can repair oxidative [DNA damage](/wiki/DNA_damage_(naturally_occurring) "DNA damage (naturally occurring)") by mechanisms analogous to those occurring in the [cell nucleus](/wiki/Cell_nucleus "Cell nucleus"). The proteins employed in [mtDNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") repair are encoded by nuclear [genes](/wiki/Gene "Gene"), and are translocated to the mitochondria. The [DNA repair](/wiki/DNA_repair "DNA repair") pathways in mammalian mitochondria include [base excision repair](/wiki/Base_excision_repair "Base excision repair"), double-strand break repair, direct reversal and [mismatch repair](/wiki/DNA_mismatch_repair "DNA mismatch repair").[[196]](#cite_note-Gredilla-2012-196)[[197]](#cite_note-Saki-2017-197) Alternatively, DNA damage may be bypassed, rather than repaired, by translesion synthesis.

Of the several DNA repair process in mitochondria, the base excision repair pathway has been most comprehensively studied.[[197]](#cite_note-Saki-2017-197) Base excision repair is carried out by a sequence of enzyme-catalyzed steps that include recognition and excision of a damaged DNA base, removal of the resulting abasic site, end processing, gap filling and ligation. A common damage in mtDNA that is repaired by base excision repair is [8-oxoguanine](/wiki/8-oxoguanine "8-oxoguanine") produced by oxidation of [guanine](/wiki/Guanine "Guanine").[[198]](#cite_note-Leon-2016-198)

Double-strand breaks can be repaired by [homologous recombinational](/wiki/Homologous_recombination "Homologous recombination") repair in both mammalian mtDNA[[199]](#cite_note-Dahal-2018-199) and plant mtDNA.[[200]](#cite_note-Odahara-2007-200) Double-strand breaks in mtDNA can also be repaired by [microhomology-mediated end joining](/wiki/Microhomology-mediated_end_joining "Microhomology-mediated end joining").[[201]](#cite_note-Tadi-2016-201) Although there is evidence for the repair processes of direct reversal and mismatch repair in mtDNA, these processes are not well characterized.[[197]](#cite_note-Saki-2017-197)

### Lack of mitochondrial DNA

Some organisms have lost mitochondrial DNA altogether. In these cases, genes encoded by the mitochondrial DNA have been lost or transferred to the nucleus.[[153]](#cite_note-Chan-2006-153) *[Cryptosporidium](/wiki/Cryptosporidium "Cryptosporidium")* have mitochondria that lack any DNA, presumably because all their genes have been lost or transferred.[[202]](#cite_note-Henriquez-2005-202) In *Cryptosporidium*, the mitochondria have an altered [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") generation system that renders the parasite resistant to many classical mitochondrial [inhibitors](/wiki/Enzyme_inhibitor "Enzyme inhibitor") such as [cyanide](/wiki/Cyanide "Cyanide"), [azide](/wiki/Azide "Azide"), and [atovaquone](/wiki/Atovaquone "Atovaquone").[[202]](#cite_note-Henriquez-2005-202) Mitochondria that lack their own DNA have been found in a marine parasitic [dinoflagellate](/wiki/Dinoflagellate "Dinoflagellate") from the genus *[Amoebophrya](/wiki/Amoebophrya "Amoebophrya")*. This microorganism, *A. cerati*, has functional mitochondria that lack a genome.[[203]](#cite_note-203) In related species, the mitochondrial genome still has three genes, but in *A. cerati* only a single mitochondrial gene — the [cytochrome c oxidase I](/wiki/Cytochrome_c_oxidase_subunit_I "Cytochrome c oxidase subunit I") gene (*cox1*) — is found, and it has migrated to the genome of the nucleus.[[204]](#cite_note-204)

## Dysfunction and disease

### Mitochondrial diseases

Main article: [Mitochondrial disease](/wiki/Mitochondrial_disease "Mitochondrial disease")

Damage and subsequent dysfunction in mitochondria is an important factor in a range of human diseases due to their influence in cell metabolism. Mitochondrial disorders often present as neurological disorders, including [autism](/wiki/Autism "Autism").[[18]](#cite_note-Griffiths-2017-18) They can also manifest as [myopathy](/wiki/Myopathy "Myopathy"), [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), multiple [endocrinopathy](/wiki/Endocrinopathy "Endocrinopathy"), and a variety of other systemic disorders.[[205]](#cite_note-Zeviani-2004-205) Diseases caused by mutation in the mtDNA include [Kearns–Sayre syndrome](/wiki/Kearns%E2%80%93Sayre_syndrome "Kearns–Sayre syndrome"), [MELAS syndrome](/wiki/MELAS_syndrome "MELAS syndrome") and [Leber's hereditary optic neuropathy](/wiki/Leber%27s_hereditary_optic_neuropathy "Leber's hereditary optic neuropathy").[[206]](#cite_note-Taylor-2005-206) In the vast majority of cases, these diseases are transmitted by a female to her children, as the [zygote](/wiki/Zygote "Zygote") derives its mitochondria and hence its mtDNA from the ovum. Diseases such as Kearns-Sayre syndrome, [Pearson syndrome](/wiki/Pearson_syndrome "Pearson syndrome"), and progressive external [ophthalmoplegia](/wiki/Ophthalmoparesis "Ophthalmoparesis") are thought to be due to large-scale mtDNA rearrangements, whereas other diseases such as MELAS syndrome, Leber's hereditary optic neuropathy, [MERRF syndrome](/wiki/MERRF_syndrome "MERRF syndrome"), and others are due to [point mutations](/wiki/Point_mutation "Point mutation") in mtDNA.[[205]](#cite_note-Zeviani-2004-205)

It has also been reported that drug tolerant cancer cells have an increased number and size of mitochondria which suggested an increase in mitochondrial biogenesis.[[207]](#cite_note-Goldman-2019-207) A 2022 study in *Nature Nanotechnology* has reported that cancer cells can hijack the mitochondria from immune cells via physical tunneling nanotubes.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

In other diseases, defects in nuclear genes lead to dysfunction of mitochondrial proteins. This is the case in [Friedreich's ataxia](/wiki/Friedreich%27s_ataxia "Friedreich's ataxia"), [hereditary spastic paraplegia](/wiki/Hereditary_spastic_paraplegia "Hereditary spastic paraplegia"), and [Wilson's disease](/wiki/Wilson%27s_disease "Wilson's disease").[[209]](#cite_note-209) These diseases are inherited in a [dominance relationship](/wiki/Dominance_relationship "Dominance relationship"), as applies to most other genetic diseases. A variety of disorders can be caused by nuclear mutations of oxidative phosphorylation enzymes, such as [coenzyme Q10](/wiki/Coenzyme_Q10 "Coenzyme Q10") deficiency and [Barth syndrome](/wiki/Barth_syndrome "Barth syndrome").[[205]](#cite_note-Zeviani-2004-205) Environmental influences may interact with hereditary predispositions and cause mitochondrial disease. For example, there may be a link between [pesticide](/wiki/Pesticide "Pesticide") exposure and the later onset of [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease").[[210]](#cite_note-210)[[211]](#cite_note-211) Other pathologies with etiology involving mitochondrial dysfunction include [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia"), [bipolar disorder](/wiki/Bipolar_disorder "Bipolar disorder"), [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia"), [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"),[[212]](#cite_note-212)[[213]](#cite_note-213) Parkinson's disease, [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), [cardiovascular disease](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease"), [myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome](/wiki/Myalgic_encephalomyelitis/chronic_fatigue_syndrome "Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome") (ME/CFS), [retinitis pigmentosa](/wiki/Retinitis_pigmentosa "Retinitis pigmentosa"), and [diabetes mellitus](/wiki/Diabetes_mellitus "Diabetes mellitus").[[214]](#cite_note-214)[[215]](#cite_note-Pieczenik-2007-215)

Mitochondria-mediated oxidative stress plays a role in cardiomyopathy in [type 2 diabetics](/wiki/Type_2_diabetics "Type 2 diabetics"). Increased fatty acid delivery to the heart increases fatty acid uptake by cardiomyocytes, resulting in increased fatty acid oxidation in these cells. This process increases the reducing equivalents available to the electron transport chain of the mitochondria, ultimately increasing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production. ROS increases [uncoupling proteins](/wiki/Uncoupling_proteins "Uncoupling proteins") (UCPs) and potentiate proton leakage through the [adenine nucleotide translocator](/wiki/Adenine_nucleotide_translocator "Adenine nucleotide translocator") (ANT), the combination of which [uncouples](/wiki/Uncoupler "Uncoupler") the mitochondria. Uncoupling then increases oxygen consumption by the mitochondria, compounding the increase in fatty acid oxidation. This creates a vicious cycle of uncoupling; furthermore, even though oxygen consumption increases, ATP synthesis does not increase proportionally because the mitochondria are uncoupled. Less ATP availability ultimately results in an energy deficit presenting as reduced cardiac efficiency and contractile dysfunction. To compound the problem, impaired sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium release and reduced mitochondrial reuptake limits peak cytosolic levels of the important signaling ion during muscle contraction. Decreased intra-mitochondrial calcium concentration increases dehydrogenase activation and ATP synthesis. So in addition to lower ATP synthesis due to fatty acid oxidation, ATP synthesis is impaired by poor calcium signaling as well, causing cardiac problems for diabetics.[[216]](#cite_note-216)

Mitochondria also modulate processes such as testicular somatic cell development, spermatogonial stem cell differentiation, luminal acidification, testosterone production in testes, and more. Thus, dysfunction of mitochondria in spermatozoa can be a cause for infertility.[[217]](#cite_note-217)

In efforts to combat mitochondrial disease, [mitochondrial replacement therapy](/wiki/Mitochondrial_replacement_therapy "Mitochondrial replacement therapy") (MRT) has been developed. This form of in vitro fertilization uses donor mitochondria, which avoids the transmission of diseases caused by mutations of mitochondrial DNA.[[218]](#cite_note-218) However, this therapy is still being researched and can introduce genetic modification, as well as safety concerns. These diseases are rare but can be extremely debilitating and progressive diseases, thus posing complex ethical questions for public policy.[[219]](#cite_note-219)

### Relationships to aging

See also: [Hallmarks of aging § Mitochondrial dysfunction](/wiki/Hallmarks_of_aging#Mitochondrial_dysfunction "Hallmarks of aging")

There may be some leakage of the [electrons transferred](/wiki/Electron_transport_chain "Electron transport chain") in the respiratory chain to form [reactive oxygen species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species"). This was thought to result in significant [oxidative stress](/wiki/Oxidative_stress "Oxidative stress") in the mitochondria with high mutation rates of mitochondrial DNA.[[220]](#cite_note-220) Hypothesized links between aging and oxidative stress are not new and were proposed in 1956,[[221]](#cite_note-221) which was later refined into the [mitochondrial free radical theory of aging](/wiki/Mitochondrial_theory_of_ageing "Mitochondrial theory of ageing").[[222]](#cite_note-222) A vicious cycle was thought to occur, as oxidative stress leads to mitochondrial DNA mutations, which can lead to enzymatic abnormalities and further oxidative stress.

A number of changes can occur to mitochondria during the aging process.[[223]](#cite_note-223) Tissues from elderly humans show a decrease in enzymatic activity of the proteins of the respiratory chain.[[224]](#cite_note-224) However, mutated mtDNA can only be found in about 0.2% of very old cells.[[225]](#cite_note-225) Large deletions in the mitochondrial genome have been hypothesized to lead to high levels of [oxidative stress](/wiki/Oxidative_stress "Oxidative stress") and neuronal death in [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease").[[226]](#cite_note-226) Mitochondrial dysfunction has also been shown to occur in [amyotrophic lateral sclerosis](/wiki/Amyotrophic_lateral_sclerosis "Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis").[[227]](#cite_note-227)[[228]](#cite_note-228)

Since mitochondria cover a pivotal role in the ovarian function, by providing ATP necessary for the development from germinal vesicle to mature [oocyte](/wiki/Oocyte "Oocyte"), a decreased mitochondria function can lead to inflammation, resulting in premature ovarian failure and accelerated ovarian aging. The resulting dysfunction is then reflected in quantitative (such as mtDNA copy number and mtDNA deletions), qualitative (such as mutations and strand breaks) and oxidative damage (such as dysfunctional mitochondria due to ROS), which are not only relevant in ovarian aging, but perturb oocyte-cumulus crosstalk in the ovary, are linked to genetic disorders (such as Fragile X) and can interfere with embryo selection.[[229]](#cite_note-229)

## History

The first observations of intracellular structures that probably represented mitochondria were published in 1857, by the physiologist [Albert von Kolliker](/wiki/Albert_von_K%C3%B6lliker "Albert von Kölliker").[[230]](#cite_note-230)[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231) [Richard Altmann](/wiki/Richard_Altmann "Richard Altmann"), in 1890, established them as cell organelles and called them "bioblasts".[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231)[[232]](#cite_note-232) In 1898, Carl Benda coined the term "mitochondria" from the [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") μίτος, *mitos*, "thread", and χονδρίον, *chondrion*, "granule".[[233]](#cite_note-OnlineEtymDict-233)[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231)[[234]](#cite_note-234) [Leonor Michaelis](/wiki/Leonor_Michaelis "Leonor Michaelis") discovered that [Janus green](/wiki/Janus_green "Janus green") can be used as a [supravital stain](/wiki/Supravital_stain "Supravital stain") for mitochondria in 1900.[[235]](#cite_note-235) In 1904, [Friedrich Meves](/w/index.php?title=Friedrich_Meves&action=edit&redlink=1 "Friedrich Meves (page does not exist)") made the first recorded observation of mitochondria in plants in cells of the white waterlily, *[Nymphaea alba](/wiki/Nymphaea_alba "Nymphaea alba"),*[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231)[[236]](#cite_note-236) and in 1908, along with [Claudius Regaud](/wiki/Claudius_Regaud "Claudius Regaud"), suggested that they contain proteins and lipids. Benjamin F. Kingsbury, in 1912, first related them with cell respiration, but almost exclusively based on morphological observations.[[237]](#cite_note-237)[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231) In 1913, [Otto Heinrich Warburg](/wiki/Otto_Heinrich_Warburg "Otto Heinrich Warburg") linked respiration to particles which he had obtained from extracts of guinea-pig liver and which he called "grana".[[238]](#cite_note-238) Warburg and [Heinrich Otto Wieland](/wiki/Heinrich_Otto_Wieland "Heinrich Otto Wieland"), who had also postulated a similar particle mechanism, disagreed on the chemical nature of the respiration. It was not until 1925, when [David Keilin](/wiki/David_Keilin "David Keilin") discovered [cytochromes](/wiki/Cytochromes "Cytochromes"), that the [respiratory chain](/wiki/Respiratory_chain "Respiratory chain") was described.[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231)

In 1939, experiments using minced muscle cells demonstrated that cellular respiration using one [oxygen molecule](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") can form four [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (ATP) molecules, and in 1941, the concept of the phosphate bonds of ATP being a form of energy in cellular metabolism was developed by [Fritz Albert Lipmann](/wiki/Fritz_Albert_Lipmann "Fritz Albert Lipmann"). In the following years, the mechanism behind cellular respiration was further elaborated, although its link to the mitochondria was not known.[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231) The introduction of [tissue fractionation](/wiki/Cell_fractionation "Cell fractionation") by [Albert Claude](/wiki/Albert_Claude "Albert Claude") allowed mitochondria to be isolated from other cell fractions and biochemical analysis to be conducted on them alone. In 1946, he concluded that [cytochrome oxidase](/wiki/Cytochrome_oxidase "Cytochrome oxidase") and other enzymes responsible for the respiratory chain were isolated to the mitochondria. [Eugene Kennedy](/wiki/Eugene_P._Kennedy "Eugene P. Kennedy") and [Albert Lehninger](/wiki/Albert_L._Lehninger "Albert L. Lehninger") discovered in 1948 that mitochondria are the site of [oxidative phosphorylation](/wiki/Oxidative_phosphorylation "Oxidative phosphorylation") in eukaryotes. Over time, the fractionation method was further developed, improving the quality of the mitochondria isolated, and other elements of [cell respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration") were determined to occur in the mitochondria.[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231)

The first high-resolution electron [micrographs](/wiki/Micrographs "Micrographs") appeared in 1952, replacing the Janus Green stains as the preferred way to visualize mitochondria.[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231) This led to a more detailed analysis of the structure of the mitochondria, including confirmation that they were surrounded by a membrane. It also showed a second membrane inside the mitochondria that folded up in ridges dividing up the inner chamber and that the size and shape of the mitochondria varied from cell to cell.

The popular term "powerhouse of the cell" was coined by [Philip Siekevitz](/wiki/Philip_Siekevitz "Philip Siekevitz") in 1957.[[4]](#cite_note-Siekevitz-1957-4)[[239]](#cite_note-239)

In 1967, it was discovered that mitochondria contained [ribosomes](/wiki/Ribosomes "Ribosomes").[[240]](#cite_note-240) In 1968, methods were developed for mapping the mitochondrial genes, with the genetic and physical map of yeast mitochondrial DNA completed in 1976.[[231]](#cite_note-Ernster-1981-231) In November 2024, Researchers from the United States have discovered that mitochondria divide into two distinct forms when cells are starved, this could help explain and describe how cancers thrive in hostile conditions.[[241]](#cite_note-241)

Kölliker said (p. 321) that he had found mitochondria in the muscles of other animals. In Figure 3 of [Table XIV](https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/49190#page/564/mode/1up), Kölliker depicted mitochondria in frog muscles.
